Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd usually wait until I had a chunk of free time, maybe on weekends or between shifts at work, and then try to cram in what I could. Being a kinesthetic learner, I found it hard to just sit and read for hours, so I'd break things up by doing practice problems or walking around while listening to lectures. With limited resources, I couldn't always study when I wanted to because I'd be at the library using their computers or waiting for wifi access.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because work picked up or I had other responsibilities. When deadlines hit, I'd suddenly find myself putting in more hours, but it wasn't consistent at all. I think I averaged around 16 hours a week, but that was spread unevenly throughout the semester.
Q3: Usually a deadline would prompt me to log in, or sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post or an assignment. I wasn't the type to just browse the LMS for fun or out of curiosity. If there wasn't something specific I needed to do, I probably wouldn't log in. Sometimes I'd check just to make sure I wasn't missing something important, but that was about it.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine. I'd use it when I had to, but it always felt like a chore rather than something automatic. I think if the platform had been more interactive or hands-on, I might have engaged more naturally. It just never clicked as a habit for me.
Q5: The videos were actually pretty helpful because I could watch them and then try to apply what I learned right away. I watched quite a bit of video content because I could pause, rewind, and actually do things with my hands while listening. The discussion posts were okay too because interacting with peers helped me understand different perspectives, even if I didn't post as much as I could have.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary resources. They felt dry and passive, which doesn't work well for how I learn. I'd skim them at best or just skip to the parts that seemed directly relevant to assignments. If something wasn't going to be tested or wasn't clearly connected to what I needed to submit, I probably didn't prioritize it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could actually do something with it, like practice problems or interactive exercises. If it was just reading or watching without any application, I'd get bored fast. I also valued things that directly connected to assignments or exams because with my limited time and resources, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones had a deadline and consequences, so I'd make sure to at least attempt them. Optional stuff? I'd think about doing it but then something else would come up and I'd never get around to it. There's only so much time in the day, and when you're juggling work and school with limited resources, optional doesn't make the cut.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download materials when I had wifi access and then study them offline later. I also used my own notes a lot, writing things out by hand because that helps me remember better. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos or other external resources that explained concepts in a more hands-on way. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were many times when I was studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd print out materials at the library and work through them at home or on the bus. I'd practice concepts physically, like making models or doing calculations on paper. I also studied with classmates in person, working through problems together, which obviously doesn't show up in any log.
Q11: Yeah, that was pretty common for me. I'd access a video or document once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never go back to the LMS version. It was more practical that way since I didn't always have reliable internet. Once I had what I needed, I'd work with it offline for days or even weeks.
Q12: Around midterms things got really hard because work scheduled me for extra shifts and I just couldn't keep up. I also hit a point where the material felt repetitive and boring, which made it tough to motivate myself. When you're already not super motivated and then life gets busy, engagement drops fast. The CBT session I did actually helped me reframe some of that, which was nice.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and stare at a screen. Competing responsibilities were huge for me. I had to prioritize paying bills over studying sometimes, which meant my LMS usage would tank during those periods. Fatigue made everything feel harder than it needed to be.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd open a video or reading and just zone out completely. My eyes would be on the screen but my brain was somewhere else, thinking about work or what I needed to do later. I'd technically have accessed the material but I couldn't tell you what it said if you asked me right after.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was boredom, honestly. The tasks didn't excite me, so I'd push them off until the pressure built up. I also avoided things that seemed confusing because I didn't want to deal with the frustration. It's easier to avoid than to struggle through something when you're already tired.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only reason I logged in regularly. Without a deadline, I had no urgency. When something was due, I'd suddenly become very active, downloading materials, submitting work, checking requirements. My LMS usage was almost entirely deadline-driven, which probably explains why my engagement looked low overall but I still managed to pass.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change my behavior much, to be honest. By the time I got feedback, I'd already moved on to the next thing. If the feedback was positive, I felt okay about it. If it was critical, I'd feel a bit down but wouldn't necessarily go back and redo anything. It just didn't feel urgent enough to act on.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard, which didn't help or hurt me much. Having clear due dates helped me know when to engage. But the lack of hands-on or interactive elements made it harder for someone like me who learns by doing. If there had been more practical components, I probably would have been more involved.
Q19: The heavy reliance on reading and passive video content made engagement harder for me. I need to move, to do things with my hands, to apply concepts practically. The discussion forums were okay but felt forced sometimes. What would have helped is more interactive simulations or projects where I could actually build or create something.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but struggling to actually complete the work. Like, they might watch videos and read materials looking for understanding, but then can't translate that into finished assignments. Or maybe they're logging in hoping to feel motivated but never actually getting there. It's like being present but not productive.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I didn't log in much, but I studied offline quite a bit and managed to pass with decent exam scores. The LMS activity doesn't capture what I was doing with downloaded materials or my handwritten notes. You can learn effectively without constantly being online, especially if you know how to study independently.
Q22: A course log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show whether you actually understood anything. It misses offline studying, physical note-taking, group study sessions, and whether you were mentally present when you accessed something. It's like measuring attendance at a gym but not whether anyone actually exercised.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting effort into understanding it, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively doing something with the material, applying it, questioning it, making it my own. It's not about how many times I log in but whether my brain is actually working on the content.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through hands-on practice away from the computer, or how the CBT session actually improved my mood and helped me push through the boring parts. It wouldn't capture my conversations with classmates or the creative ways I made the material stick despite being bored. My engagement was real, just not always visible online.
